meta.title: 5.2 GraphQL and gRPC
meta.description: When REST is not enough. GraphQL for flexible queries, gRPC for high-performance service-to-service calls.

blocks.prompt: A mobile app needs to display a user's profile with their name, avatar, 3 most recent posts, and follower count. With REST, this might require 3 API calls. Is there a better way?

blocks.content:
## GraphQL

A query language for APIs. Clients specify exactly what data they need in a single request.

```graphql
query {
  user(id: 123) {
    name
    avatar
    posts(limit: 3) { title, createdAt }
    followerCount
  }
}
```

**Solves:** Over-fetching (REST returns entire objects), under-fetching (need multiple REST calls), and the N+1 problem for related data.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Caching is harder (queries are dynamic, not URL-based)
- Rate limiting is complex (one query can be cheap or expensive)
- Backend complexity increases (resolver chains, N+1 in resolvers)
- Monitoring is harder (all requests go to one endpoint)

**Best for:** Mobile apps (bandwidth-sensitive), frontend teams that need flexibility, APIs with complex relational data.

## gRPC

Google's RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers.

```protobuf
service UserService {
  rpc GetUser(GetUserRequest) returns (User);
  rpc ListUsers(ListUsersRequest) returns (stream User);
}
```

**Advantages over REST:**
- 2-10x smaller payloads (binary Protobuf vs JSON)
- Code generation for type-safe clients in any language
- Streaming support (server, client, bidirectional)
- HTTP/2 multiplexing built-in

**Tradeoffs:**
- Browser support requires gRPC-Web proxy
- Not human-readable (binary format)
- Harder to debug with curl

**Best for:** Internal service-to-service calls, mobile clients (bandwidth efficiency), streaming use cases.

## When to use what

| Scenario | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Public-facing API | REST | Universal, well-understood, cacheable |
| Mobile app with complex data | GraphQL | Flexible queries, minimal bandwidth |
| Service-to-service (internal) | gRPC | Fast, typed, streaming |
| Browser real-time | WebSockets/SSE | Bidirectional or server push |
| Simple CRUD with few clients | REST | Simplicity wins |

problems.question: A microservices platform has 50 internal services that communicate heavily. Each service team maintains its own API contracts. Services are written in Go, Java, and Python. Which API technology would provide the most benefit for internal communication?
options: A) REST with OpenAPI specifications for documentation
B) gRPC with Protocol Buffers for type-safe, language-agnostic contracts
C) GraphQL with a federated schema across all services
problems.explanation: gRPC with Protobuf is ideal for internal service-to-service communication across multiple languages. It provides: (1) type-safe code generation in Go, Java, and Python from shared .proto files, (2) 2-10x better performance than JSON/REST (binary serialization, HTTP/2), (3) streaming support for real-time data, (4) backward-compatible schema evolution. REST with OpenAPI (A) would work but lacks code generation quality and performance. GraphQL (C) is designed for client-facing flexibility, not service-to-service — it adds unnecessary complexity for internal calls.

blocks.content: GraphQL lets clients request exactly the data they need — ideal for mobile and complex UIs. gRPC provides high-performance, type-safe service-to-service communication with code generation. REST remains the default for public APIs. Choose based on your use case, not hype.

blocks.prompt: APIs are synchronous: call and wait. Module 5.3 introduces asynchronous patterns — webhooks and event-driven APIs where the server pushes data when ready.
